content.text: A mesh topology connects agents selectively: each agent can communicate directly with specific peers, but not every agent is connected to every other. Unlike fully decentralized systems where all agents share the same message bus, a mesh has explicit, curated connections — an adjacency map that defines exactly which agents can talk to which. This combines the flexibility of peer communication with the intentionality of a designed topology.

attrs.alt: Network/Mesh topology diagram showing 5 agents with selective peer connections

content.text: Mesh vs Fully Decentralized

In a fully decentralized system, every agent subscribes to a shared bus and any agent can publish to any topic. In a mesh, connections are explicit: you define an adjacency map at startup, and agents can only call peers they are wired to. This prevents accidental coupling and makes the communication graph auditable — you can draw it as a diagram and reason about it.
Property
Decentralized (Full Bus)
Mesh (Selective Peers)
Connection model
Any agent → any topic
Explicit adjacency map
Coupling risk
High — any agent can affect any other
Low — only wired pairs interact
Auditability
Hard to trace who calls whom
Adjacency map is the spec
Flexibility
Easy to add new subscribers
Requires updating adjacency to wire new agents
Debugging
Must trace all topic subscriptions
Follow the edges in the adjacency map
Implementing a Mesh With an Adjacency Map

The simplest mesh implementation is a dictionary mapping agent names to the set of agent names they are allowed to call. A router function checks the adjacency map before forwarding a call, rejecting unauthorized cross-agent calls at runtime.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Callable, Awaitable, Any
from anthropic import AsyncAnthropic

AgentFn = Callable[[str, AsyncAnthropic], Awaitable[str]]

@dataclass
class MeshRouter:
    """Routes inter-agent calls according to an explicit adjacency map."""
    agents: dict[str, AgentFn]
    adjacency: dict[str, set[str]]   # caller → allowed callees

    async def call(
        self,
        caller: str,
        callee: str,
        payload: str,
        client: AsyncAnthropic,
    ) -> str:
        allowed = self.adjacency.get(caller, set())
        if callee not in allowed:
            raise PermissionError(
                f"Agent {caller!r} is not wired to {callee!r}. "
                f"Allowed: {sorted(allowed)}"
            )
        agent_fn = self.agents.get(callee)
        if agent_fn is None:
            raise KeyError(f"Unknown agent: {callee!r}")
        return await agent_fn(payload, client)

# Define the mesh
async def research_agent(task: str, client: AsyncAnthropic) -> str:
    resp = await client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001", max_tokens=512,
        system="Retrieve facts.", messages=[{"role": "user", "content": task}],
    )
    return resp.content[0].text

async def writer_agent(context: str, client: AsyncAnthropic) -> str:
    resp = await client.messages.create(
        model="claude-sonnet-5", max_tokens=2048,
        system="Write a report.", messages=[{"role": "user", "content": context}],
    )
    return resp.content[0].text

async def reviewer_agent(draft: str, client: AsyncAnthropic) -> str:
    resp = await client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001", max_tokens=512,
        system="Review for clarity.", messages=[{"role": "user", "content": draft}],
    )
    return resp.content[0].text

mesh = MeshRouter(
    agents={
        "research": research_agent,
        "writer": writer_agent,
        "reviewer": reviewer_agent,
    },
    adjacency={
        "research": {"writer"},         # research feeds writer only
        "writer":   {"reviewer"},       # writer sends drafts to reviewer only
        "reviewer": set(),              # reviewer is a sink — no outbound calls
    },
)

async def run(query: str, client: AsyncAnthropic) -> str:
    chunks  = await mesh.call("research",  "writer",   query,  client)
    draft   = await mesh.call("writer",    "reviewer", chunks, client)
    # reviewer is a sink; its output is the final result
    return draft
Mesh Topology in LangGraph With Command Handoffs

LangGraph's Command pattern implements mesh handoffs directly: each node returns a Command specifying exactly which peer node receives control next. The adjacency is implicit in the goto values — you cannot route to a node that is not in the graph. This gives compile-time enforcement of the mesh structure.

from langgraph.graph import StateGraph, END
from langgraph.types import Command
from langchain_anthropic import ChatAnthropic
from typing import TypedDict, Literal

class MeshState(TypedDict):
    query: str
    chunks: str
    draft: str
    review: str

llm = ChatAnthropic(model="claude-sonnet-5")

def research_node(state: MeshState) -> Command[Literal["writer"]]:
    resp = llm.invoke([
        {"role": "system", "content": "Retrieve key facts."},
        {"role": "user", "content": state["query"]},
    ])
    # Only wired to writer — enforced by Literal type hint
    return Command(update={"chunks": resp.content}, goto="writer")

def writer_node(state: MeshState) -> Command[Literal["reviewer"]]:
    resp = llm.invoke([
        {"role": "system", "content": "Write a report from these facts."},
        {"role": "user", "content": state["chunks"]},
    ])
    # Only wired to reviewer
    return Command(update={"draft": resp.content}, goto="reviewer")

def reviewer_node(state: MeshState) -> dict:
    resp = llm.invoke([
        {"role": "system", "content": "Review for clarity and accuracy."},
        {"role": "user", "content": state["draft"]},
    ])
    # Sink node — routes to END
    return {"review": resp.content}

graph = StateGraph(MeshState)
graph.add_node("research", research_node)
graph.add_node("writer", writer_node)
graph.add_node("reviewer", reviewer_node)
graph.set_entry_point("research")
graph.add_edge("reviewer", END)
app = graph.compile()
Dynamic Mesh Rewiring

A static adjacency map works for most cases, but some systems need to change which agents are connected at runtime — for example, routing to a backup agent when the primary is at capacity, or enabling a new connection path after a model upgrade is verified. Dynamic rewiring updates the adjacency map without restarting the system.

import asyncio
from dataclasses import dataclass, field

@dataclass
class DynamicMeshRouter:
    agents: dict
    adjacency: dict[str, set[str]] = field(default_factory=dict)
    _lock: asyncio.Lock = field(default_factory=asyncio.Lock)

    async def wire(self, caller: str, callee: str) -> None:
        """Add a connection at runtime (thread-safe)."""
        async with self._lock:
            self.adjacency.setdefault(caller, set()).add(callee)

    async def unwire(self, caller: str, callee: str) -> None:
        """Remove a connection at runtime."""
        async with self._lock:
            self.adjacency.get(caller, set()).discard(callee)

    async def call(self, caller: str, callee: str, payload: str, client) -> str:
        async with self._lock:
            allowed = frozenset(self.adjacency.get(caller, set()))
        if callee not in allowed:
            raise PermissionError(f"{caller!r} → {callee!r} not wired")
        return await self.agents[callee](payload, client)

# Usage: swap writer_v2 in without downtime
# await router.unwire("research", "writer_v1")
# await router.wire("research",   "writer_v2")
Mesh Topology Use Cases
Peer review pipelines: each specialist reviews only the output of its direct predecessor
Multi-stage validation: fact-checker and style-checker both connect to writer, but not to each other
Failover meshes: primary agent wired to downstream; backup agent wired to same downstream, switched on failure
Collaborative debate: two analyst agents wired to each other for back-and-forth refinement, then both wired to a merger
Domain-separated systems: legal, finance, and tech specialists each wired to a shared formatter, but not to each other
Testing Mesh Topology — Verifying Adjacency Rules

The adjacency map is executable documentation. Write tests that assert unauthorized connections raise errors, and that authorized connections succeed. This locks in the intended communication graph and catches accidental coupling when new agents are added.
Cycle Detection in a Mesh

A mesh with a cycle — Agent A calls B, B calls C, C calls A — produces an infinite loop. Cycles are usually bugs, not intentional design. A simple depth-first search over the adjacency map at startup detects cycles before any agent runs.
Choosing Among All Four Topologies

Each topology optimizes for a different constraint. The decision usually comes down to whether you need a global merge point, how many agents communicate with each other, and how important it is that each agent fails independently. Most production systems start centralized and introduce mesh or hierarchical patterns only as complexity demands it.

def recommend_topology(
    needs_global_merge: bool,
    num_agents: int,
    agents_react_to_events: bool,
    need_selective_peer_calls: bool,
) -> str:
    """Simple decision heuristic for topology selection."""
    if agents_react_to_events:
        return "decentralized"          # pub/sub is natural fit

    if need_selective_peer_calls and num_agents > 4:
        return "mesh"                   # explicit adjacency beats full bus

    if not needs_global_merge and num_agents > 8:
        return "hierarchical"           # too many workers for one orchestrator

    return "centralized"                # simplest; start here

# Common scenarios
print(recommend_topology(True,  5, False, False))   # centralized
print(recommend_topology(False, 12, False, False))  # hierarchical
print(recommend_topology(False, 6,  False, True))   # mesh
print(recommend_topology(False, 4,  True,  False))  # decentralized
Summary
Mesh topology defines explicit connections between agent pairs — unlike a full bus, not every agent can reach every other
An adjacency map is the spec: it defines who talks to whom and serves as both configuration and documentation
LangGraph Command(goto=...) with Literal type hints gives compile-time adjacency enforcement
Dynamic rewiring updates the adjacency at runtime without restarting — useful for failover and gradual rollouts
Use mesh when peer-to-peer flexibility is needed but you want to avoid the accidental coupling of a full shared bus
Prefer mesh over hierarchical when domains are not cleanly tiered but each agent has a limited, known set of partners
